findings.title: sga-revenue-trend / working-capital-trend silently treat non-consecutive valid years as consecutive when computing slope
findings.description: Both new Tag 214a / 215d methods build `sgaRatios` / `wcRatios` by iterating positions 0..WINDOW-1 (latest-first) and pushing only valid (SGA,Rev>0) or (CA,CL,Rev>0) pairs with `continue` on missing. The resulting array is reversed and fed to `_slope(ys)` which treats x=[0,1,...] as consecutive year indices. If position 1 is missing but 0 and 2 are valid, the function plugs years 0 and 2 into x=[0,1] — slope is computed as if a 2-year gap were a 1-year change. With WINDOW=3 the worst case is one missing middle year, but if either Math.min picks shorter inputs across sgaArr/revArr/balArr or a 3-year-window has a gap, the slope sign and magnitude are wrong. Pattern fix is the year-positional discipline already applied in earnings-stability/q-spike-dataguard (raw arrays + explicit i-tracking).
findings.mechanism: _slope uses x = 0..n-1 sequential indices, not actual year offsets. After the `continue` skip, the ratio at position 2 becomes ys[1] (treated as year t-1).
findings.reproducer: annualSGA = [{value: 1.0}, null, {value: 0.6}]; annualRev = [{value: 10}, {value: 12}, {value: 8}]. sgaRatios = [0.10, 0.075] from positions 0 and 2 only. _slope of chrono=[0.075, 0.10] => slope = +0.025/yr (2.5pp/yr — would FAIL +0.5pp/yr threshold). True per-year delta from t-2 to t-0 is (0.10-0.075)/2 = +0.0125/yr (1.25pp/yr) — half the apparent severity. Reverse-direction examples yield false PASS instead of false FAIL.
findings.impact: False-positive failures (and false-negative passes) on tickers with one gap year in the 3-year window. DIAGNOSTIC pp/yr values shown in modes-report are misleading. Bounded by WINDOW=3 today but Tag 211l snapshot coverage is still rolling out and gaps are common.
findings.suggested_fix: Track positional year-offset alongside each valid ratio: `ratios.push({i, ratio})`. Pass explicit (x,y) pairs to a generalized linear regression OR hard-require 3 CONSECUTIVE valid years (positions 0..2 all valid), returning incomputable otherwise — matches the intent of '3y slope'.

findings.title: fcf-conversion-stability description still says '>= 0.85' but THRESHOLD constant is 0.75 since Tag 215b
findings.description: module.exports.description: 'FCF/NetIncome 5y geometric mean >= 0.85 — cash-conversion persistence' but THRESHOLD = 0.75 (lowered in Tag 215b to fix MSFT/GOOG false-fails). The description flows into Runner.getMethods() and downstream into modes-report tooltips and method-chip titles. Operators reading the tooltip see '>= 0.85' while the underlying gate is 0.75 — a stock at 0.78 advertises 'fail' (vs 0.85) but actually passes. Pure documentation drift, not a logic bug, but causes operator confusion on borderline cases.
findings.mechanism: THRESHOLD constant updated in Tag 215b without updating the module.exports.description string. Both are exported but only one was kept in sync.
findings.reproducer: Runner.getMethods().find(m => m.id === 'fcf-conversion-stability').description returns the stale '>= 0.85' phrasing. Any stock with geomean in [0.75, 0.85) has components.thresholds.geomeanFloor = 0.75 but tooltip claims 0.85.
findings.impact: Operator confusion in modes-report and any UI consumer that surfaces method.description. Audit / manual review may flag passing stocks at 0.78 as 'should have failed'. Low real impact but trivially fixable.
findings.suggested_fix: Update description string to '>= 0.75' and add a comment noting it must stay in lockstep with THRESHOLD. Even better: refactor description to interpolate `THRESHOLD` directly so it cannot drift on future tag-bumps.

findings.title: score-aggregator normalizeMethodScore does not handle 'lt' / 'gt' threshold ops — falls through to default 0.3
findings.description: normalizeMethodScore handles only `gte`, `lte`, `lte_abs` and returns 0.3 for any other op. ohlson-o-score exports thresholdOp='lt'; operating-leverage-margin-accel exports 'gt'; revenue-quality-cov exports 'lt'. All three are DIAGNOSTIC and not in SCORE_WEIGHTS today — so scoring is not currently affected, but display side-effects ARE: _helpers.js _inferConfidence and _autoFlags only special-case 'lte_abs', so NEAR_THRESHOLD-style flags and confidence boosts are subtly miscomputed for 'lt'/'gt' methods today. More urgent: if any of these are promoted to SCORE_WEIGHTS (ohlson was already promoted to DATAGUARD in Tag 224b), the graduated near-pass behaviour collapses to flat 0.3 silently.
findings.mechanism: Strict equality check on op string. 'lt' / 'gt' fall through the if/else-if chain to `return 0.3`. Same coverage gap in _helpers.js confidence / flag inference.
findings.reproducer: Add 'ohlson-o-score': 0.1 to SCORE_WEIGHTS.TURNAROUND. Stock with ohlson O = -0.2 (just-failing O<0): pass=false (since op='lt'). normalizeMethodScore returns 0.3 flat — no graduation regardless of proximity. Same stock with thresholdOp manually rewritten to 'lte': would correctly graduate via the lte branch.
findings.impact: Latent — currently no SCORE_WEIGHTS entry uses 'lt'/'gt'. Becomes real bug the moment any method with these ops is added to weights. Also degrades the NEAR_THRESHOLD signal TODAY for ohlson/op-leverage-margin-accel/rev-quality-cov display.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend normalizeMethodScore to handle 'lt' (mirror 'lte' with open-interval pass semantics already enforced by pass=) and 'gt' (mirror 'gte'). Mirror the same coverage in _helpers.js _inferConfidence and _autoFlags. Add a console.warn at module load if any registered method exports an unrecognized op.

findings.title: rule-of-x does not apply rule-of-40 Tag-201c '3y annual median fcfMargin' fallback — inconsistent WC-noisy TTM treatment across HG cores
findings.description: rule-of-40 (Tag 201c) deliberately falls back to a 3y annual median FCF-margin when TTM is negative AND the annual median is materially positive (the MELI fix — handles WC-noisy quarter). rule-of-x consumes the same growth and fcfMargin inputs but does NOT apply the fallback, using `1.5*growth + fcfMarginTTM` directly. A stock can therefore PASS rule-of-40 (via fallback path) but FAIL rule-of-x using the same reporting period. Both are in SCORE_WEIGHTS.HYPERGROWTH (weights 0.25 and 0.10 respectively).
findings.mechanism: Tag 201c patch applied only to rule-of-40.js. rule-of-x.js was left on the original H.metricValue path.
findings.reproducer: Stock with metrics.revenueGrowthYoY=30, metrics.fcfMarginTTM=-13 (MELI-ish), annualFCF/annualRev producing 3y median margin ~33%. rule-of-40: fallback fires, value = 30 + 33 = 63 → PASS. rule-of-x: no fallback, value = 1.5*30 + (-13) = 32 → FAIL. Same stock scored as 'PASS' on R40 weight 0.25 and ~0.3 partial on Rx weight 0.10.
findings.impact: MELI-pattern compounders score inconsistently across the two HG-core methods. Aggregate HG score is slightly reduced. Fix is mechanical.
findings.suggested_fix: Move `_annualFcfMarginMedian` from rule-of-40 to a shared helper (or _helpers.js). Apply identical fallback inside rule-of-x. Document the lockstep contract in a comment.

findings.title: revenue-shock-guard quarterly path: filter() collapses null gaps, breaking positional alignment of the prior-quarter MAD window
findings.description: Tag 184 (F-ME-025) fixed the case where qrev[0] is null (fall through to annual). But the next line `const windowAll = qrev.slice(0, 8); const window = windowAll.filter(v => Number.isFinite(v));` still applies a filter that collapses internal nulls. Then `prior = window.slice(1)` is fed to _median/_mad as if it were consecutive quarters. If positions 2 and 5 are null but the rest present, prior becomes [Q1, Q3, Q4, Q6, Q7] — a non-peer mix. MAD over this set is inflated (broader temporal spread), z-score deflated, and genuine shocks can slip past the 4.5 threshold.
findings.mechanism: filter() reindexes the array. _median and _mad operate on values agnostic to position, but z-score requires the prior window to be the temporal peer set of the latest quarter.
findings.reproducer: timeseries.revenueQ = [{value:1000},{value:200},null,{value:180},{value:175},null,{value:170},{value:165}]. windowAll len 8, post-filter window len 6 = [1000,200,180,175,170,165]. prior=[200,180,175,170,165]. Median≈180, MAD≈5. z=(1000-180)/(1.4826*5)≈110 (shock caught). But if latest = 220 (moderate jump in normal cadence) and one of the prior includes a temporally-distant quarter, MAD increases, z drops below 4.5 — shock missed.
findings.impact: Edge-case shocks missed on tickers with sporadic Yahoo reporting gaps. The absoluteJump materiality check is built on the same potentially-misaligned prior median. Modest exposure for US large-caps (rare gaps) but real for international and recent IPOs.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the same raw-positional discipline used for qrev[0]: walk qrev[1..k] forward until you collect N CONSECUTIVE finite values, then run median/MAD on that contiguous window. Don't filter the slice.

findings.title: earnings-surprise-momentum can pass on only 3 valid surprises but description says '>= 3 of last 4'
findings.description: MIN_QUARTERS=3 (incomputable below 3) and POSITIVE_COUNT_FLOOR=3. When only 3 of the last 4 quarters have a finite surprisePercent, breadthPass becomes `positiveCount >= 3` against a sample of 3 — i.e. requires 3 of 3 beats. Method then reports value=3 against threshold=3 with pass=true. The advertised '>= 3 of last 4' silently degrades to '>= 3 of last 3 (1 missing)' for thin-data quarters — simultaneously stricter (100% beats required) and more lenient (the 4th quarter's potential miss never enters the count).
findings.mechanism: POSITIVE_COUNT_FLOOR is an absolute count, not relative. Comparing against a denominator that varies from 3 to 4 silently shifts the implicit pass ratio from 100% to 75%.
findings.reproducer: earningsHistory with 4 entries where one has surprisePercent=null and three are positive: surprises.length=3, positiveCount=3, breadthPass=true. magnitudePass also passes if mean>=3. Stock passes with a 3-of-3 record at the relaxed sample, even though the missing quarter could have been -10% miss.
findings.impact: DIAGNOSTIC so no direct score impact, but the modes-report chip claims '>=3/4' is met when the underlying evidence is '3 of 3 (1 missing)'. Pre-Tag-220c snapshots universally return incomputable, so very few tickers hit this path TODAY — activates broadly after next full pull.
findings.suggested_fix: Compute breadthPass against the FIXED denominator WINDOW (4), not surprises.length. Or require surprises.length === WINDOW to emit pass=true, returning incomputable with reason 'thin coverage' otherwise.

findings.title: betting-against-beta stores BORDERLINE indicator at components.flag (singular) — wrapEvaluate reads result.flags (plural array)
findings.description: evaluate() stores the BORDERLINE indicator at `components.flag` ('BORDERLINE' | null). _helpers.js wrapEvaluate concatenates auto-flags with `Array.isArray(raw.flags) ? raw.flags : []` — i.e. reads result.flags (plural, top-level array). There is NO read path that surfaces components.flag, so the borderline marker is dead-information for any downstream consumer (modes-report card chips, audit-trail, UI badges).
findings.mechanism: Singular `flag` key inside components vs. expected plural `flags` array at the top-level result. wrapEvaluate's flag concat reads from the top level, not nested.
findings.reproducer: Run evaluate() on a stock with beta=1.2 (borderline). result.components.flag === 'BORDERLINE'. result.flags is undefined. wrapEvaluate's final flags = [] (only auto-flags). Modes-report has no chip for the borderline state — operator cannot distinguish (1.0,1.5] BORDERLINE from >1.5 FAIL.
findings.impact: Pure information loss. Both bands have pass=false but BORDERLINE is documented (in Tag 230b header) as 'much weaker negative evidence than > 1.5'. Operators reading 'FAIL' get no signal that beta is 1.2 vs 2.5.
findings.suggested_fix: Emit `flags: borderline ? ['BORDERLINE'] : []` as a top-level result field (return key inside H.buildResult or directly). Optionally also keep components.flag for backward audit-trail symmetry. The canonical surface for downstream consumers is result.flags.

findings.title: reinvestment-rate substitutes 0 for missing annualRnD[j] while the asset-light fallback is gated on rnd.length>0 — partial R&D coverage gets the strict 20% threshold with a deflated denominator
findings.description: Inside the ratio-building loop, `var r = (j < rawRnd.length && Number.isFinite(rawRnd[j])) ? rawRnd[j] : 0;` substitutes 0 for missing R&D years. The asset-light fallback (threshold 0.10) requires `!usedRnD = !(rnd.length > 0)` — i.e. NO R&D years anywhere. If even ONE year of annualRnD is present, the method uses 0-substitution for the other years AND demands the strict 20% threshold. A software/IP-heavy firm with R&D persisted only for year 0 computes capex+0 for years 1-4, badly understating reinvestment intensity and likely false-failing.
findings.mechanism: Boolean usedRnD ignores per-year coverage. Mixed coverage (some years have R&D, some don't) silently uses 0-substitution AND the strict 20% threshold simultaneously.
findings.reproducer: annualCapex = 5y of {value:1B}; annualRnD = [{value:5B}, null, null, null, null]; annualOCF = 5y of {value:10B}. ratios[0]=(1+5)/10=0.60; ratios[1..4]=(1+0)/10=0.10. median=0.10. assetLight=false (rnd.length=1). Method FAILS at 0.10 vs 0.20 floor — even though plausibly the firm spends ~$5B/yr on R&D and Yahoo simply didn't persist years 1-4.
findings.impact: False-fails R&D-heavy QC-MUST methods when annualRnD coverage is partial. Cascading impact on QC mode passability for software compounders. Real impact moderate — Yahoo usually persists R&D contiguously, but mixed coverage exists.
findings.suggested_fix: Track per-year R&D availability. If a year has capex but no R&D, either (a) skip that year entirely (year-alignment discipline), (b) impute via trailing-median R&D, OR (c) trigger asset-light fallback on per-year coverage instead of array length. Option (a) is most conservative and matches the broader year-alignment fixes elsewhere.

findings.title: normalizeMethodScore early threshold===0 guard returns 0.99 for any val>0 — wrong semantics for lte/lt/lte_abs methods with threshold===0
findings.description: Line 114: `if (threshold === 0) return val > 0 ? 1.0 : 0.0;` runs BEFORE the per-op dispatch. Assumes op='gte' semantics (val>0 = better than threshold). For a hypothetical method with op='lte_abs' threshold=0 ('|value| should be ~0') this returns 1.0 for ANY positive value — the opposite of intended. Comment at line 128 acknowledges this as dead code today (no method exports lte_abs+threshold-0) but flags latent risk. ohlson-o-score uses op='lt' threshold=0 — currently dispatches to the unhandled-op return 0.3 (per F-ME-003), MASKING this. If F-ME-003 is fixed by adding 'lt' support, the early threshold===0 return would then claim val=-5 returns 0.0 (val>0 is false) — but op='lt' wants val<0 = pass, so the correct answer is 1.0.
findings.mechanism: The threshold===0 early-return predates op-aware logic and assumes 'gte' semantics. Other ops would need different sign treatment (lte: val<=0=better; lt: val<0=better; lte_abs: |val|=0=best).
findings.reproducer: Manually call normalizeMethodScore({computable:true, pass:false, value:-3, threshold:0, thresholdOp:'lt'}, {threshold:0, thresholdOp:'lt'}). Returns 0.0 (because -3>0 is false). Should return ~1.0 (very negative O-score = healthy bankruptcy probability). For val=+3 (failing case), returns 1.0 — should return 0.0.
findings.impact: Latent risk. Activates as soon as a method with threshold===0 and op!='gte' is added to SCORE_WEIGHTS. ohlson promotion to DATAGUARD didn't trigger this (DATAGUARDs don't enter scoring), but future composite weighting of ohlson would silently invert its score contribution.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the threshold===0 guard INSIDE the per-op branches. In 'gte': val>0 → 1.0, val<=0 → 0.0. In 'lte': val<=0 → 1.0, val>0 → 0.0. In 'lte_abs': |val|<=eps → 1.0, else 0.0. In 'lt'/'gt' (per F-ME-003 fix): mirror with strict open intervals.

findings.title: hypergrowth-quality-class annual fallback scales strongQ/solidQ assuming all 3 annual pairs evaluated — under- and over-counts when middle year is null
findings.description: When revQ length<8, falls back to annual revY and counts YoY pairs (i=0..2). Inside the loop, both cur and prev are null-guarded so pairs with missing data are skipped. Then lines 95-98 hard-code: `strongQ === 3 → 4; strongQ === 2 → 3` (similar for solidQ). The scaling assumes all 3 annual pairs were evaluated, but the loop is conditional. With one annual gap (e.g. revY position 2 null), only 1-2 pairs are evaluated. A stock with 2-of-2 strong annual pairs gets strongQ=2 → 3 after scaling, claiming '3-of-4 strong quarterly equivalent' on 2-of-2 annual evidence — OVERSTATES breadth. Conversely a stock with 1-of-2 strong evaluated pairs gets strongQ=1 (no scaling for <2) — UNDERSTATES on tickers with one annual gap.
findings.mechanism: Scaling logic is positional (assumes 3 attempted pairs) but the loop is conditional (skips nulls). The two are out of sync.
findings.reproducer: revY = [{value:1000},{value:500},null,{value:300}] (length 4, passes filter check). Pairs evaluated: (0,1)=100% strong, (1,2)=skip, (2,3)=skip. strongQ=1, solidQ=1. Scaling leaves both at 1. Classification falls into HYPERGROWTH_REVIEW — too weak for a stock that doubled YoY. Inverse: revY = [{v:1000},{v:500},{v:300},{v:200}] full: 3 strong pairs → strongQ=3 → 4 (REAL_HYPERGROWTH). Same true growth profile but partial data → wrong classification.
findings.impact: False-positive REAL_HYPERGROWTH classifications when 2-of-2 valid pairs scale up; false-negative HYPERGROWTH_REVIEW when 1-of-2 doesn't scale. hypergrowth-quality-class is in SCORE_WEIGHTS.HYPERGROWTH at weight 0.15, so real scoring impact. Recent IPOs and post-spinoff names with one annual gap are most exposed.
findings.suggested_fix: Scale by ACTUAL valid pair count, not by the loop variable: track `pairsEvaluated` and after the loop apply `if (pairsEvaluated > 0) { scaleFactor = 4 / pairsEvaluated; strongQ = Math.round(strongQ * scaleFactor); solidQ = Math.round(solidQ * scaleFactor); }`. OR require all 3 pairs valid and emit incomputable otherwise.

findings.title: profitability-state stores stringly-typed value at components.confidence (e.g. '3/3 NI+OI+FCF') — shadows the documented numeric confidence convention
findings.description: _helpers.js _inferConfidence checks `result.components.confidence` and uses it as a 0..1 numeric override when Number.isFinite. profitability-state writes a STRING ('3/3 NI+OI+FCF') to that exact same key. The string is correctly ignored by the finite-check, so behavior is benign today — _inferConfidence falls through to the default 0.7. But the key collision means any future method that legitimately wants to set a numeric confidence override would clash with profitability-state's display semantics, and any reader iterating components.confidence without type-checking gets polymorphic data.
findings.mechanism: Same key name used for two different concepts: 'confidence-tag display string' vs 'numeric confidence override per _helpers.js contract'.
findings.reproducer: Inspect stock result: results['profitability-state'].components.confidence === '3/3 NI+OI+FCF' (string). Any UI consumer doing `Number(c).toFixed(2)` returns 'NaN'. Any consumer relying on the _helpers.js confidence-override contract is silently bypassed by the string value (safe via finite-check today, fragile under refactor).
findings.impact: Operator UI weirdness if anyone surfaces components.confidence directly. Latent fragility — easy to misread in code review.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename profitability-state's display key to components.sourceConfirmation. Reserve components.confidence for the numeric override contract documented by _helpers.js. Add a runner-level assertion that asserts `typeof components.confidence === 'number'` when present.

findings.title: magic-formula EV synthesis treats null totalDebt as 0 — silently understates EV for debt-bearing firms with Yahoo balance-sheet gap
findings.description: When metrics.enterpriseValue is missing/invalid, the method synthesizes EV = marketCap + totalDebt - totalCash. totalCash is REQUIRED (line 166: Number.isFinite(tc)), but totalDebt falls back to 0 when null. A firm with substantial debt but a missing totalDebt line (Yahoo balance-sheet shape gap — same shape that causes ASML/AAPL/MA to return computable=false on currentAssets/currentLiabilities per the method's own anchor notes) gets EV understated by the actual debt amount. EarningsYield = EBIT/EV is then OVERSTATED, pushing composite UP — false-positive magic-formula passes.
findings.mechanism: Defensive `Number.isFinite(td) ? td : 0` defaults missing debt to zero. The header comment justifies this for 'genuine net-debt-free firms' but cannot distinguish 'truly debt-free' from 'Yahoo didn't persist the line'.
findings.reproducer: Stock with EBIT=10B, mcap=200B, totalCash=10B, totalDebt=null (Yahoo gap). EV = 200+0-10 = 190B; EY = 5.3%; eyScore = saturate(0.053, 0.08) = 0.66. Reality if debt were $50B: EV=240B, EY=4.2%, eyScore=0.52. Composite shifts ~0.07 lower — at the 0.75 threshold this flips pass/fail.
findings.impact: DIAGNOSTIC (not in SCORE_WEIGHTS) so direct score impact is zero. BUT: method header claims 'V is the only mega-cap pass (1/10)' as calibration evidence; the EV-synthesis fallback runs on multiple anchors per the same comment trail. Some 'COMPUTABLE-FAIL' anchors might be hiding 'silent PASS via debt-understatement', causing operators to mistrust the factor's calibration.
findings.suggested_fix: When totalDebt is null AND metrics.enterpriseValue is unavailable, emit computable=false with reason 'cannot determine debt — EV synthesis incomplete'. Alternative: track via components.evSourceQuality = 'mcap-only-no-debt' so the audit-trail surfaces the assumption. Conflating 'true debt-free' with 'Yahoo gap' is the actual problem.

findings.title: data-quality grading uses 'metrics.fcfMarginTTM' field name — verify pull-yahoo writer key matches reader
findings.description: data-quality.js CRITICAL_FIELDS includes `{id:'metrics.fcfMarginTTM', weight:1.0}`. rule-of-40.js and rule-of-x.js read via H.metricValue(stock, 'fcfMarginTTM'). Worth verifying that pull-yahoo actually writes `metrics.fcfMarginTTM` (vs e.g. `metrics.fcfMargin` or `metrics.freeCashflowMarginTTM`). If writer/reader names disagree, every snapshot loses 1.0 weight = ~8% of nanRatio just from this entry — silently downgrading average grade from A toward B without any underlying data problem. Same verify-and-pin discipline should be applied across every CRITICAL_FIELDS entry.
findings.mechanism: Case-sensitive key lookup with no shared constant. If writer uses 'fcfMargin' and reader uses 'fcfMarginTTM', the data-quality check fails silently per stock.
findings.reproducer: Grep pull-yahoo.js for 'fcfMarginTTM'. If only writes 'fcfMargin' without TTM suffix, every snapshot's gradeSnapshot will record `metrics.fcfMarginTTM` in missingFields regardless of actual data presence.
findings.impact: DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE is opt-in by env (currently off in production), so live scoring is unaffected. BUT: data-quality-report.js and audit-reports surface inflated 'missing field' counts. Latent risk: if Karl flips DATAQUALITY_ENFORCE=1, grade-C cap would fire on stocks that actually have complete data, capping passing tickers at NEAR_MISS.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a shared METRIC_KEYS constant module that both pull-yahoo and data-quality import. Add a unit test that loads a canonical 'gold-stock' fixture and asserts every CRITICAL_FIELDS check returns true — would catch typos at registration time.

findings.title: sector-medians-compute: OTHER sub-profile stocks fill regional buckets and are pruned post-compute — no operator-visible diagnostic when a region falls back to _GLOBAL
findings.description: gatherBySubProfile pushes every classified stock into globalBuckets[sp.id] AND regionalBuckets[region][sp.id], including sp.id === 'OTHER'. _computeFromBuckets then SKIPS OTHER via line 120 `continue` and the result is pruned of empty sub-profiles. Net effect: a region whose stocks ALL classify as OTHER produces an empty regional result, gets dropped by the length check at line 176, and effectiveThreshold falls through to _GLOBAL — which is the intended behavior. BUT there is no operator-visible diagnostic surface for 'this region's stocks all landed in OTHER, hence using _GLOBAL'. Audit / debugging this requires re-running classifySubProfile manually.
findings.mechanism: OTHER is correctly treated as 'classifier inconclusive' and excluded from threshold output (Tag-49). The gather-then-prune pattern is correct in spirit but emits no visibility.
findings.reproducer: Run computeMedians with all stocks classified as OTHER for region X. globalBuckets.OTHER and regionalBuckets[X].OTHER populated. _computeFromBuckets returns {} for both. Output: byRegion._GLOBAL = {}, byRegion.X dropped. Stocks in region X use _GLOBAL fallback (correct) but operators have no signal that OTHER classification was the cause.
findings.impact: Low — current sub-profile classifier rarely emits OTHER for the live universe. Behavior is technically correct but debuggability is poor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `_diagnostics: { otherStocksByRegion: { US: N, EU: M } }` to the computeMedians output to surface the OTHER counts. Non-invasive (additive field), useful for ops, would have caught any future regression where the classifier breaks for a specific region.

files_skipped: methods/method-types.js (helper, no logic of interest examined)
~50 other methods not sampled per scope guidance
